front: Philosopher: argued that 'good' is an irreducible property like the color yellow, and introduced the 'naturalistic fallacy' for attempts to define it in natural terms
Philosopher: attacked a utilitarian philosopher for committing the naturalistic fallacy by equating 'desirable' with 'desired,' as in the analogy between being 'visible' and being 'desirable'
Concept: the principle that definitions of 'good' cannot be both correct and informative — for any definition, one can always ask 'but is it really good?'
back: Open Question Argument / Paradox of Analysis (G.E. Moore)

front: Philosopher: inverted a classical skeptical argument by holding up a hand and saying 'here is one hand' to prove the existence of an external world
work: Proof of an External World & A Defence of Common Sense

front: Concept: changing a modus ponens to a modus tollens, a technique coined by William Rowe and named for this philosopher
work: Proof of an External World & A Defence of Common Sense

front: Concept: sentences like 'it's raining, but I don't believe it's raining' are logically consistent but pragmatically absurd
Philosopher: advocated 'ideal utilitarianism' (with Hastings Rashdall), according to which more than just pleasure should be maximized
